title: The Scaling Challenge
concept: Scaling Transformers to billions of parameters requires specialized distributed training across clusters of GPUs.

exercises.prompt: Why can't we train giant AI models on a single standard graphics card (GPU)?

options.text: They are too large to fit in a single GPU's memory.
They do not fit on standard computer screens.
They require internet access to do basic math.

exercises.explain: A single computer chip has a hard limit on how much information it can hold. Giant AI models exceed this limit, requiring multiple chips.

cards.title: Too Big for One Computer
cards.body: Modern AI models are absolutely massive, with billions of parameters. They are far too big to fit inside a single computer or graphics card (GPU). How do we train these digital giants without melting our hardware? We have to team up!

cards.title: What is Distributed Training?
cards.body: To train massive AI models, we use 'Distributed Training'. This means we connect hundreds or thousands of GPUs (special graphics chips) together into a 'cluster'. We then split the model's math and data among all these chips so they can work together as one giant supercomputer.

cards.title: The Lego Castle Crew
cards.body: Imagine building a massive Lego castle. If you try to build it all by yourself on one small table, you will run out of space and hands. But if you invite ten friends, give each person one section to build on their own table, and then connect them at the end, you can build a giant castle in no time! That is how we train giant AI models using a cluster of computers.

cards.title: Inside the AI Factory
cards.body: When companies train state-of-the-art chatbots, they don't use a normal laptop. They use thousands of interconnected GPUs in giant data centers. The AI's billions of settings (parameters) are split up, with different GPUs calculating different parts of the network at the exact same millisecond.

cards.title: Myth: Just Wait Longer!
cards.body: Many people think that to train a bigger AI, you just need to wait longer on your home computer. In reality, giant models are physically too large to fit into a single GPU's memory at all. Without splitting the model across multiple computers, training cannot even start!

cards.title: Single GPU vs. GPU Cluster
cards.body: Compare how we handle small models versus giant modern AI models.

left.text: Holds the entire small model and all training data at once.

right.text: Splits the giant model and data across hundreds of connected chips.

exercises.prompt: What happens if you try to load a giant model with 100 billion parameters onto a single standard GPU?

options.text: The model trains much faster than normal.
The computer runs out of memory and crashes.
The model automatically shrinks itself to fit.

exercises.explain: Standard computer memory (RAM or GPU memory) is limited. Billion-parameter models are simply too large to fit onto one chip, causing an 'out of memory' error.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In distributed training, the GPUs must constantly talk to each other to coordinate their work.
exercises.explain: Distributed training requires constant communication between GPUs to share what they have learned, making fast network cables essential.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe a group of connected GPUs.
exercises.sentence: To train giant AI models, we link many GPUs together into a network called a cluster.
exercises.explain: A cluster is a group of connected computers or GPUs that work together as a single powerful system to handle massive tasks.

exercises.prompt: Identify the explanation that correctly describes how we scale up AI training:

options.text: To scale Transformers, we use distributed training. This splits the model parameters and training data across a cluster of multiple GPUs, allowing them to calculate updates in parallel.
Transformers scale up by using bigger batteries in our laptops so they can run for three weeks without stopping.
To train large models, we just need to write the code in capital letters so the computer knows it is a big project.

exercises.explain: The first option is generated by an AI assistant giving a helpful, correct explanation of distributed training. The others are incorrect or irrelevant.

exercises.prompt: How do engineers successfully train massive AI models with billions of parameters without running out of computer memory?

options.text: By buying one super-sized GPU that is the size of a whole room.
By splitting the model and data across a connected network of multiple GPUs.
By shrinking the AI model until it fits onto a standard smartphone.
By running the training process slower on a single computer's CPU.

exercises.explain: Just like a large group of people building a giant tower, training a massive AI model requires dividing the work (both the data and the model parameters) across a network (cluster) of multiple GPUs. A single GPU does not have enough memory to hold billions of parameters at once.
